assignment_description:
➜ NOTE TO STUDENTS
One purpose of composition lessons is to encourage you, the student, to dig deeper, reflect on your readings, explore the different ways words work, and learn how to ask good questions of yourself and your readings. Set your timer and use the whole time allotted for a composition assignment. If you finish an assignment with extra time, explore another perspective, ask further questions, or take a bit more time to see if any other thoughts come to mind. Alternatively, if you find yourself stuck, you may want to take a short break and come back later! Growth, not frustration, is the goal.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #1 - Summary/Reflection
Write a brief summary of a current event. 
OR
Tell of a time when your desire for society was met. How did that experience impact/affect you?

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #2 - Descriptive
Describe the village of Grand-Pré from Evangeline. 
OR 
Describe the land of Greece.

• Note: Start by giving all the details you can remember from your readings. Then you may look back at your book to refresh your memory on more details to add.

• REFERENCES
Comp #1
Ethics: Part 2 (Lesson 1)
Book: Ourselves

Comp #2
Poetry Reading: Grade 8 (Lessons 3-4)
Book: Evangeline
OR 
Ancient History (G7-9) 
(Lesson 1)
Book: Ancient Greek World
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER TIP
Make sure students are practicing typing skills and typing narrations and compositions regularly.

assignment_description:
Introduction
⍞ Materials: Grammar Voyage
 
➜ RECAP
What do you remember about grammar from last year? Can you recite the eight parts of speech?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Note that "Intro" means into, and "duct" means lead. Grammar Voyage p.4-9

➜ PRACTICE
Find the end rhyme on p.5-6 and discuss what you like about the poem. Copy the 4 ways of thinking about language on p.9. Find new places on p.8-9 on a map.

Word Fragments
⍞ Materials: Grammar Voyage
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Voyage p.10

➜ PRACTICE
Copy the list of Latin words or word fragments into your notebook. Which ones do you already know? Which ones do you need to memorize? Work through all the words at the bottom of the page, substituting the meaning from the list for the highlighted part of the word. See if you can puzzle out how understanding the fragment's meaning sheds light on the meaning of the words.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #1 - Expository
Tell of a time when you took a "share in the interests of the great world" by listening with all your heart to someone who shared about a profession, skill, subject, or experience with which you were unfamiliar.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #2 - Letter
Write a letter to a family member, friend, or imaginary character about one of your favorite books so far this year. Tell them what you think the book is about and why you like it so far, and then send the letter if you like!

• REFERENCE
Comp #1
Ethics: Part 2 (Lesson 2)
Book: Ourselves
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER TIP
Some students may find it helpful to use external supports before writing, such as index cards that can be arranged and re-ordered. You can also encourage students to talk aloud as they write or use speech-to-text and then edit.

assignment_description:
Nouns
⍞ Materials: Grammar Voyage
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Voyage p.11-17

➜ PRACTICE
Copy the diagram on p.15. Discuss the size and placement of the elements.

For each noun in purple on p.16, is it a person, place, or thing?

Copy the types of nouns in purple from p.17 and come up with your own example for each type. Discuss compound nouns, too.

A marine chronometer is a clock that is accurate enough to be used to calculate longitude via celestial navigation..

There are also COMPOUND NOUNS that combine words. They are sometimes hyphenated: mother-in-law, schoolteacher, toothpaste.

Pronouns
⍞ Materials: Grammar Voyage
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Voyage p.18-21

➜ PRACTICE
Copy the charts for the two cases of pronouns on p.20. What is the plural third person subject pronoun? What is the singular first person object pronoun? What is the singular and plural second person subject and object pronoun?

Copy the lists of demonstrative, possessive, interrogative, and indefinite pronouns on p.21.

In modern usage, they and them have been adopted as singular pronouns that can replace a singular noun. For example: In the dictation lesson, the student will choose words that are unfamiliar to "them" (instead of saying "her or him"). If your child doesn't know French, "they" can start learning it this year. (instead of "he or she").

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #1 - Expository/Research
From your reading in Ourselves, compare and contrast the desire for knowledge and curiosity.
OR
(Canadian Students) Hudson Bay Company recently filed for creditor protection and began closing and liquidating (selling off the assets) of all its stores, effectively closing the business. There is currently (April 2025) a debate over whether or not the company should be allowed to auction off the charter to recoup some funds. This is a great opportunity to think about how events of long-ago history can continue to be felt in the present day. With a parent's supervision, research what has happened to the charter since April 2025 and decide which side of the debate you would support.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #2 - Opinion
From your reading in current events, what do you think are some issues our country has to deal with today? Why?
OR
(Canadian Students Only) Building on Assignment #1, prepare a list of statements supporting your position on the Hudson Bay Charter debate.

• REFERENCES
Comp #1
Ethics: Part 2 (Lesson 3)
Book: Ourselves
OR
Canadian History (G7-8)
(Lesson 6)
Book: Hudson's Bay Company Adventures

assignment_description:
Pronouns, Adjectives
⍞ Materials: Grammar Voyage
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Voyage p.22-27

➜ DISCUSS
Why is it important to make sure that the pronoun's antecedent is clear? Why is it better to say "modify" instead of "describe" to explain what adjectives do? How do adjectives modify pronouns?

Emphasize the word modify because it is much more accurate than the word describe. When the adjective happens, the noun goes poof. Example: LADDER and CORPORATE LADDER

Articles, Review
⍞ Materials: Grammar Voyage
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Voyage p.28-30

➜ PRACTICE
Find nouns, pronouns, and adjectives in the first paragraph of Moby Dick on p.29. [See Answer Key] Remember that articles are special adjectives! Find all the nouns in the passage on p.30. [31 excluding the infinitive "to ebb"] How many are plural?

This would make a good small-group activity for a classroom setting, letting students work together to find as many as they can. This presents the opportunity to discuss items they did not see or to discuss why an item they chose is none of the three.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #1 - Short Account
From your reading in Ourselves, explain how the love of knowledge is smothered by emulation. Include "marks" and "places" in your examples.
OR
From your reading in Ancient History, write an expository paragraph explaining why the Greeks stopped accepting government by oligarchs.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #2 - Point of View
Pretend you are Miriam and write a diary entry telling about recent events in Calico Captive.

• REFERENCES
Comp #1
Ancient History (G7-9, Lesson 4)
Book: Ancient Greek World
OR
Ethics: Part 2 (Lesson 4)
Book: Ourselves

Comp #2
General Literature (G7-8, Lesson 4)
Book: Calico Captive

Possessive Adj. and Pro.
⍞ Materials: Grammar Voyage
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Voyage p.31-36

➜ PRACTICE
Copy the list of possessive pronouns and adjectives. Write a sentence for 3 pronouns and 3 adjectives. Discuss the difference so that it is clear when you meet them in a sentence which is which.

Complete the exercises on p.32 and 36 [Answer Key].

This is a key writing point: needless adjectives bloat a sentence with dull syllables, making it wordy and boring. Try to find the right noun to avoid having to modify it.

Verbs
⍞ Materials: Grammar Voyage

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Voyage p.37-42

➜ DISCUSS
Which of the action verbs on p.38 do you think is most full of energy?

Note the -ing words in the 2nd-4th lines on p.39. They are not verbs but adjectives made out of verb forms. We will learn more about them later.

Craft some sentences of your own that show passive and active voice.

Verbum was the Latin word for word.

The Richard Reiss was actually a Great Lakes ship and would not have made the voyage described, but this is art, and strange things happen.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #1 - Letter
(Canadian Students) Write a letter from Pierre-Esprit Radisson back to his French fur-trade backers from La Compagnie du Nord, describing what has taken place.
Remember that by now, Radisson and des Groseilliers had switched their allegiance from England back to France.
OR
Write a letter from Macbeth to a distant and trusted friend telling what has happened so far.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #2 - Visual/Poetry
Retell a favorite chapter or scene in one of your readings by creating a comic-strip style retelling of it, OR by retelling it as a poem.

• Alternate Option: Tell of a time when you have thrown yourself into your work, and it was a delight to you. OR Tell of a character in a book who has done so, and the result was joy.

• REFERENCES
Comp #1
Canadian History (G7-8, Lesson 9)
Book: Hudson's Bay Company Adventures
OR
Shakespeare
Book: Macbeth

Comp #2
Alternate Option
Ethics: Part 2 (Lesson 5)
Book: Ourselves

Verbs
⍞ Materials: Grammar Voyage
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Voyage p.43-48

➜ PRACTICE
Copy the 3 regular tenses with their progressive forms and the 3 perfect tenses. Write examples of your own.

➜ DISCUSS
Tense is a hard concept to grasp. It will be revisited in future study. For now, note the -ing ending on the verb and a form of "being" in the progressive form and the use of a form of "have" and the past verb in the perfect tense.

LINKS means EQUALS.

A Tense Tale
⍞ Materials: Grammar Voyage
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Voyage p.49

➜ PRACTICE
This poem uses the grammar of verb tense as a basis for its structure. Write your own Verb Tense Poem.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #1 - Point of View
Imagine you are Gabriel and tell the story of the leaving from his perspective.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #2 - Compare/Contrast
From your reading in the Ancient Greek World, write a paragraph comparing children's lives in ancient Greece to children's lives today. How are they similar and different?
OR
From your reading in Calico Captive, compare Miriam to her sister and/or tell how Miriam has changed since the beginning of the book. Give one or two examples.

• Tip: for option #2, feel free to look back at your books to refresh your memory.

• REFERENCES

(Lessons 22-24)
Book: Evangeline

Comp #2

OR

Verb Agreement, Adverbs
⍞ Materials: Grammar Voyage
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Voyage p.50-53

➜ PRACTICE
Identify the words that the adverbs in purple on p.52 and p.53 are modifying. Note what type of word it is. Is it a verb, an adjective, or an adverb? Note that the word "really" is a junk adverb. It almost always damages a sentence.

➜ DISCUSS
How can adverbs make sentences wordy?

Practice
⍞ Materials: Grammar Voyage
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Voyage p.54-55

➜ DISCUSS
Were any of the words in the sentences on p.55 labeled in ways you didn't understand? See if you can figure out what word the confusing word is modifying.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #1 Short Account/Poem
Describe the Spartan way of life. What do you think of it?
OR
Explain the four ways in which a person is able to recognize authentic love.
OR
Write a poem about selfless love using your own examples patterned after soldiers, mothers, nurses, missionaries, etc.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #2 - News Report
Tell about an event in a book, a recent life experience you had, or a current event you read about as if you were a reporter for the news.

• REFERENCES
Comp #1
Ancient History (G7-9, Lesson 7)
Book: Ancient Greek World

Conjunctions
⍞ Materials: Grammar Voyage
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Voyage p.56-59

➜ PRACTICE
Copy the 7 coordinating conjunctions into your notebook. Find the coordinating conjunctions in the poem on p.57. (There are 7.) Note the foggy sounds in the poem, the f's and sh's especially.

Copy the subordinating and correlative conjunctions into your notebook. Complete sentences #1-7 on p.59. [Answer Key]

Conjunctions
⍞ Materials: Grammar Voyage
 
➜ PRACTICE
Grammar Voyage p.59 Complete sentences #8-15 [Answer Key].

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Voyage p.60

➜ PRACTICE
Identify each bolded conjunction on p.60 as coordinating or subordinating.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #1 - Creative Narration/Story
Pretend you are Pierre and write a diary entry from him about his and Miriam's conversation in Ch.16-17.
OR
Retell something you read recently as a poem or as a scene in a play.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #2 - Description Review
Choose a place to sit outside. Write 5-10 sentences describing what you see, starting from your left to your right. Then write another paragraph describing the same place a different way (right to left, top to bottom, bottom to top, near to far, or another way). Which description do you like better? As you run across descriptive scenes in books or describe things yourself in science or any other subject, think about how the description is organized.

• REFERENCE
Comp #1
General Lit (G7-8, Lesson 8)
Book: Calico Captive

Prepositions
⍞ Materials: Grammar Voyage
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Voyage p.61-65

➜ PRACTICE
Copy the preposition categories on p.61. See if you can correlate the prepositions in purple on p.62 with these categories. Review your memorized subject and object pronouns.

➜ NOTE: 
On p.62, "For" in "for the passengers waited" is not in purple because it is a coordinating conjunction, not a preposition. How do we know?

Emphasize that prepositions show many kinds of relationships, not just relationships of space or time.

Review, Interjections
⍞ Materials: Grammar Voyage
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Voyage p.66-69

➜ PRACTICE
Find the prepositional phrases in the poem on p.67 [35]. Then write three sentences in which the preposition acts like an adjective and three in which it acts like an adverb.. Where does the phrase have to go if it is acting like an adjective? See if you can spot some prepositional phrases acting like adjectives in the poem.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #1 - Letter/Story
(Canadian Students) Write a newspaper article or a letter from Samuel Hearne describing the discovery of artifacts on Marble Island.
OR 
Write a fable-type short story in which a character's self-pity is exposed, and he overcomes it.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #2 - Point of View
Pick a favorite scene or part of a book you've read recently (for school or on your own time) and pretend that somehow that section of the book has been ripped out and lost. Rewrite it yourself as if you are the author,

Comp #1

Review

Pick three words from the poem on p.72 that you think are the most powerful and best chosen. What part of speech is each one?

Label each word on p.73 by writing the part of speech below it. [Answer Key]

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #1 - Dialogue/Diary
Write dialogue paragraphs where the Greek scientists discuss some of their new ideas with each other. Aim to make it about a page long.
OR
Write a diary entry by Mrs. Sergeant or Mrs. Maurice about recent happenings.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #2 - Descriptive
Describe something you've noticed outside recently in nature study. Include as many details as you can and think about how you organize your description (think of its smell, sight, sound, taste, and touch!)

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #1 - Compare/Contrast
Write a comparison paragraph comparing Pierre to Phineas. How are they similar and different?

Write a comparison paragraph comparing ancient Greek education to modern education. What similarities and differences do you see?

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #2 - Analysis
Read the beginning and ending paragraphs of Black Ships Before Troy. Then read the first several and last several lines of the direct translation of the Iliad. What do you notice about the different ways the authors begin and end the story? What would you think each story was about if you just read those opening and closing lines? Jot down your thoughts.

Complete one-level analysis on sentences #11-15 [Answer Key].
Grammar Voyage p.76

Punctuation
⍞ Materials: Grammar Voyage
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Voyage p.77

➜ NOTE
Coordinate adjectives sound fine if you change their order or insert an "and" in place of the comma. For example: A blue and short boat. The adjectives are equal and separate. Versus: Get blue and five oars. The order matters in cumulative adjectives. We say: Get five blue oars.

➜ PRACTICE
Complete the exercises p.78 [Answer Key].

Exam Week

There are no exams for this course.

assignment_description:
➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #1 - Letter
Show sympathy to someone you know by writing them a letter and sharing your interest in music, poetry, art, or anything that gives you joy.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #2 - Revision
Revision and editing is an important aspect of writing, especially when sharing it with others.

Choose a narration or composition assignment from last term that is about a page long. As you read, use different colored pencils to underline sentences by topic. Did you write a separate paragraph for each topic? If not, type a second draft. Ensure each paragraph only deals with one topic, and the sentences follow a logical order.

• REFERENCE
Comp #1
Ethics: Part 2 (Lesson 13)
Book: Ourselves

★ TEACHER TIP
Make sure students are practicing typing skills and typing narrations regularly.

assignment_description:
Writing
⍞ Materials: Grammar Voyage
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Voyage p.79-82

Subjects and Predicates
⍞ Materials: Practice Voyage, Grammar Voyage
 
➜ WARM UP
Label the parts of speech and write a comment about something you find interesting. Check your answers using the Practice Voyage Answer Key. If you missed any, figure out why and make corrections. Read the comment.
Practice Voyage "Sentence 5" p.16

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Voyage p.83-87

➜ DISCUSS
Find the missing hmmm!

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #1 - Editing
Reread your second narration draft from last week. What you did is called "revision," because you are revising content. The next thing to do is to edit. When we edit, we look for sentence structure, misplaced modifiers, subject-verb disagreement, wordiness, spelling, weak words, etc. Write a third draft to correct mistakes and make each word pack a punch. Compare with your first draft. Which is better? Why?

Remember: All good writers revise and edit their work. It's a good thing to improve your writing and to find and fix errors! Very few people write perfect sentences the first time they put their ideas on paper (or type them).

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #2 - Personification
You might have noticed that Benjamin Franklin's "wit and wisdom" in Poetry Reading is filled with personification. An author uses personification when s/he gives an inanimate object or abstract idea the qualities of a person. For instance, here is one of Franklin's aphorisms:

"Anger and Folly walk cheek by-jole; 
Repentance treads on both their Heels."

Notice how Franklin gives Anger, Folly, and Repentance human-like qualities. They are all walking! Look for more instances of personification in the books you read. With the remaining time, try writing a few sentences like Franklin's using personification. For instance, start by trying to write the opposite of Franklin's saying!
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER TIP
Some students may find it helpful to use external supports before writing, such as index cards that can be arranged and re-ordered. You can also encourage students to talk aloud as they write or use speech-to-text and then edit.

Subject Complements
⍞ Materials: Practice Voyage, Grammar Voyage

➜ WARM UP
Practice Voyage #7 p.18

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Voyage p.92-93

➜ DISCUSS
If the verb does not LINK, it is not linking. The subject IS or = the subject complement. Some linking verbs sound like action verbs until you realize that they are not describing the subject acting. To say that the ship looks rusty does not mean that the ship has eyes and is looking around.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #1 - Digging Deeper
Pick a written narration from this week that you typed and add to it. You can fill in additional details you didn't have time to include, add a section giving your thoughts on the chapter, write a series of questions you wish you could ask the author about the story, add a section telling about the events from a different character's perspective, or anything else. Narrations you do after your reading are extremely important to help you understand and think about what you read, but there is always more to discuss and learn!

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #2 - Sequence
How is egotism a foe of Sympathy? Include examples.
OR 
Tell what you did yesterday in order (you can write out your day with short sentences or bullet points, or design a visual flow chart or series of sketches by hand or with software such as Canva if desired).

• REFERENCE

Spend both your composition periods working on one of these longer assignments.

Option 1: (Canadian Students) Continue your writing exercise from Lesson 33.
OR
Option 2: In Much Ado About Nothing, Act 3, Scene 2, Don John tricks Claudio into believing that Hero is unfaithful. Imagine you have overheard Don John’s scheme before Claudio falls for the lie. Your task is to write a formal letter warning Claudio not to trust Don John and to have him arrested by the Night Watch led by Dogberry.

Your Task: Write a formal letter to Claudio, urging him not to trust Don John. Your letter should include:
\- A proper address (e.g., "Dear Claudio").
\- A clear introduction stating your purpose.
\- Reasons why he should not trust Don John, including: Actions or quotes from Don John revealing his schemes.
\- Why Hero is trustworthy and why he should marry her.
\- A closing and signature (e.g., "Sincerely, [Your Name]").

Your letter should be at least one to two paragraphs.

assignment_teachersNote:
★ Grammar/Comp: Grade 8 — TERM 2 EXAM QUESTIONS
· Grammar Voyage/Practice Voyage: In each of the following sentences, tell the part of speech for the word in brackets (noun, pronoun, adjective, verb, adverb, preposition, conjunction, or interjection).
1. The [ships] formed a row in front of the marina.
2. The captain [nailed] the doubloon on the mast.
3. [The] freighter pulled slowly to a stop.
4. A smokestack rose [high] above the top of the bridge.
5. The sailors ran quickly [up] the gangplank.
6. [Wow], the storm lasted all night.
7. They did not arrive, but we kept waiting [at] the dock.
8. [She] made the new captain a better chart.

Answers
1. noun
2. verb
3. adjective
4. adverb
5. preposition
6. interjection
7. preposition
8. pronoun

· Grammar Voyage/Practice Voyage: In the following sentences, is the bracketed word a subject, predicate, direct object, indirect object, or subject complement?
9. The people in this harbor are very [friendly].
10. John could see the new [marina] from his cabin porthole.
11. The captain gave [Susan] a pair of binoculars.
12. It was [he] who rang the ship's bell yesterday.
13. The cook gave [Roberto] a sack of hot rolls from the galley.

Answers:
9. subject complement
10. direct object
11. indirect object
12. subject complement
13. indirect object

Compound-Complex 
⍞ Materials: Practice Voyage, Grammar Voyage
 
➜ WARM UP
Practice Voyage #25 p.36

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Voyage p.140

➜ PRACTICE
Copy the diagram at the top of p.140. Write 2 compound sentences. Write 2 complex sentences. Write 2 compound-complex sentences.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #1 - Scene
Turn the first part of Treasure Island, Celebrated Cases of Judge Dee, or another book of your choice into a scene for a play! Look at one of Shakespeare's plays to see how to set up your scene. Remember that plays essentially rely on dialogue and stage directions to work. So it may even help to try acting it out or imagining how you'd set it up in a room first. Feel free to look back at your books and use excerpts of dialogue from the book as you work on your scene.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #2 - Questions
Continue working on your scene for a play.
OR
Write as many questions as you can about one of your readings. We can use the same questions for books that you might use for nature study to make observations about books and generate more questions: "What do you notice?" "What do you wonder?" and "What does it remind you of?" Don't worry if you think of a lot of questions and then draw a blank. Give yourself some time and keep thinking, and you'll probably come up with some new questions!

• REFERENCE
General Lit (G7-8, Term 3)
Book: Celebrated Cases, Treasure Island, or Book of Choice
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER TIP
Some students may find it helpful to use external supports before writing, such as index cards that can be arranged and re-ordered. You can also encourage students to talk aloud as they write or use speech-to-text and then edit.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #1 - Opinion
Pick a discussion question from a recent lesson and write your thoughts on it. Try to write one or two well-constructed paragraphs answering the question. Include connection words between them, showing how your thoughts are related to each other.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #2 - Poem/Sketch
Write a poem about a favorite scene in one of your books, OR draw/sketch a comic-strip style retelling of the same section.

Practice
⍞ Materials: Practice Voyage
 
➜ PRACTICE
Practice Voyage #30-33 p.41-44 Complete four-level analysis for the rest of the term.

➜ WRITE
Last year in Paragraph Town, you learned about descriptive paragraphs. It is a paragraph where you describe what something looks like so the reader can imagine it. It might be a place, a scene, or the way a person looks. You write the concrete details you want the reader to imagine. Think about something you want to describe. It can be something you see outside or on a walk you took in nature. You can also pick a place, scene, or person you read about in history, geography, literature, or Bible. Spend the rest of your lesson time writing your paragraph, and you can finish and revise your draft in the next lesson.

Practice
⍞ Materials: Practice Voyage
 
➜ PRACTICE
Practice Voyage #34-37 p.45-48

➜ WRITE
You can finish your descriptive paragraph if necessary. The next thing to do is to edit. When we edit, we look for sentence structure, misplaced modifiers, subject-verb disagreement, wordiness, spelling, weak words, etc. Write a second draft to correct mistakes and make each word pack a punch. Compare it with your first draft. Which is better? Why?

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #1 - Found Verse Annotation
PREP: Print the PDF Excerpt from Equiano's Narrative and/or plan to have your student use Google Docs for this lesson.
∞ PDF Link: From the Interesting Narrative
OR
∞ Link: Google Doc Template

Read the section at the end of "Nearer My Freedom" about how the authors created the poetry in the book (or use the link below if not using the book, and click on the image which tells about how the authors created the verse).
∞ Article Link: Found Verse

• Today, read through the excerpt from Equiano's life (in the PDF or on Google Docs) and highlight the words and phrases you feel are particularly important to painting the scene and understanding the story. You might want to read the excerpt through once and then go back and read it again while highlighting/underlining. Next lesson, you'll use these to create your own found verse.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #2 - Found Verse Construction
Continue with your found verse project today, either by hand or online, by taking the words and phrases you underlined or highlighted and putting them onto a separate piece of paper or a separate Google Doc. Play with the words to make your own found verse version of Equiano's chapter. Experiment with different line breaks and feel free to get rid of a phrase you had initially underlined or add a new one in. Read your work aloud to check if it flows logically and makes sense. Then read it to someone else and see what they think!

• REFERENCE
Lit/Mythology (G7-8, Lesson 18)
Book: Nearer My Freedom*

*Students do not need to be using the Lit/Mythology lesson plans to complete this assignment.

Practice
⍞ Materials: Practice Voyage
 
➜ PRACTICE
Practice Voyage #38-41 p.49-52

➜ WRITE
For the rest of the lesson, you will write a dialogue paragraph. Each character's words are put in a separate paragraph. Every time the speaker changes, you will start a new paragraph. You may use a book as a reference to help you with capitalization and punctuation rules regarding dialogue. Think of a scene you can write about from a book you have been reading in literature or history. You can finish up in the next lesson and then edit.

Practice
⍞ Materials: Practice Voyage 
 
➜ PRACTICE
Practice Voyage #42-45 p.53-56

➜ WRITE
You can finish your dialogue paragraph if necessary. The next thing to do is to edit. Look for sentence structure, misplaced modifiers, subject-verb disagreement, wordiness, spelling, weak words, etc. Write a second draft to correct mistakes and make each word pack a punch. Compare it with your first draft. Which is better? Why?

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #1 - Point of View
From your reading in Plutarch, write a composition from the point of view of the goddess Fortune, explaining her role in the story.
OR
Pretend you are Jim and write a diary entry of the day's events in Ch.13-15.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #2 - Poem/Expository

OR
Write about three causes of disloyalty (what loyalty forbids) and their effects. Give an example of each.

OR

⍞ Materials: Practice Voyage 
 
➜ PRACTICE
Practice Voyage #46-49 p.57-60

➜ WRITE
The rest of the lesson will be spent on brainstorming for your expository paragraphs. These are paragraphs that explain something. They are used for most formal essays and research papers. Think about a topic you would like to write about. What are three things you could discuss about that topic? Make a list and put them in a logical order.

Example: Dogs
--Types of dogs
--Benefits of owning a dog
--Responsibilities of owning a dog

Under each topic, list 3-7 things you want to say about it. Consider the sequence. What would be the best order to put your ideas in so that your writing is clear to a reader?

Practice
⍞ Materials: Practice Voyage
 
➜ PRACTICE
Practice Voyage #50-53 p.61-64 Be sure to check your work.

➜ WRITE 
Write your first paragraph using the outline you made last time as a guide.

Some rules for formal writing:
• Do not write in first person. (In other words, do not use the pronouns "I" or "me.")
• Do not use contractions (can't, don't, etc.)
• Do not copy or paraphrase from another work.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #1 - Draft Descriptive Paragraph
Write a descriptive paragraph about something you observed in a science lab, a nature walk, a building you studied in Architecture, or a scene from one of your other books. Focus today on writing as many details as possible. Next assignment, you'll have time to edit and revise. It's often hard to think about grammar and style ideas while also trying to get details down on paper, so we'll separate that process into two different steps, as many writers do.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #2 - Revise Descriptive Paragraph
Revise your work from Comp Assignment #1.
• Type your work if you haven't already. 
• Look at your paragraph and see if you can improve its structure and content. Does it need a topic sentence, connecting words, another sentence or two added, a sentence taken away, or moved to a different place in the paragraph? Do you have enough concrete details that someone can imagine what you're describing? Add more if not!
• Read your work aloud and check grammar and spelling.

Practice
⍞ Materials: Practice Voyage 
 
➜ PRACTICE
Practice Voyage #54-57 p.65-68

➜ WRITE
Write your second paragraph.

More Rules:
• Your topic sentence must both tell what this paragraph is about AND link back to the previous paragraph.
• Indent or skip a line to show a change in paragraphs.
• Follow the punctuation rules you have learned.

Practice
⍞ Materials: Practice Voyage 
 
➜ PRACTICE
Practice Voyage #58-61 p.69-72

➜ WRITE
Write your third paragraph. Follow the rules already discussed.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #1 - Point of View
Imagine you are a parent who is trying to help your teen recognize and overcome Pride. What advice would you give your teen? Name 10 actions you would recommend to your teen to get away from the thought of himself or herself.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #2 - Creative Narration
Write a letter to a family member or a real or imaginary friend telling them about a book you read this year and why you liked it. Give them a brief overview of what the book is about, and then tell why you enjoyed it.
OR 
Retell a scene from a recent reading as a poem, a scene from a play, or a comic strip.

• REFERENCE
Comp #1
Ethics: Part 2 (Lesson 33)
Book: Ourselves

Practice
⍞ Materials: Practice Voyage 
 
➜ PRACTICE
Practice Voyage #62-65 p.73-76

➜ WRITE
Read aloud all three paragraphs you wrote, listening for logic and flow. Does everything make sense? Would organizing in a different way make it better? Think about your word choices. Are they vivid and accurate? Have you thought of something you would like to add? Where would be the best place to put it? Check for things like spelling, capitalization, punctuation, misplaced modifiers, wordiness, etc. Use a thesaurus to find just the right words.

Begin working on your second draft. You can finish it in the next lesson.

Practice
⍞ Materials: Practice Voyage 
 
➜ PRACTICE
Practice Voyage #66-69 p.77-80

➜ WRITE
Finish your second draft. Read your paragraphs aloud to someone.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #1 -Scene/Opinion
Write a few scenes in which you show through the characters that gladness is catching.
OR
Compare the way Phillis Wheatley talks about slavery in her letter to Samuel Occam and her poem "On being brought from Africa to America." How do you think the fact that she wrote the letter after she was freed affected what she chose to say?

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #2
Catch-up Lesson, complete a previous composition assignment you missed, OR choose a discussion question from a lesson to write on.

• REFERENCES
Comp #1
Ethics: Part 2 (Lesson 34)
Book: Ourselves
OR
Poetry Reading: Grade 8 (Lesson 135) 
Book: The Poems of Phillis Wheatley

Practice
⍞ Materials: Practice Voyage 
 
➜ PRACTICE
Practice Voyage #70-73 p.81-84

➜ WRITE
You will be writing a comparison paragraph. It is a kind of expository paragraph where you explain the ways in which two or more things are similar and different—compare and contrast. Today, you will just brainstorm, thinking of two topics to write about and creating a Venn diagram that shows the similarities and differences. Try to write about two characters from history, citizenship, literature, or Bible that you read about recently. There is an example of a Venn diagram in the links.

Practice
⍞ Materials: Practice Voyage 
 
➜ PRACTICE
Practice Voyage #74-78 p.85-89

➜ WRITE
Using your Venn diagram, write a comparison paragraph showing the similarities and differences between the two characters. You can finish next week if necessary.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #1 - Tracing Argument
Summarize Equiano's argument against slavery at the end of his book. What are his main points? Feel free to use the chapter in Nearer My Freedom as well as the fuller linked excerpt. 
∞ Article Link: Nearer My Freedom

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #2 - Catch Up Lesson
Use this time to catch up on lessons in this or another course, or explore Extra Helpings.

• REFERENCE
Comp #1
Lit/Mythology (G7-8, Lesson 35)
Book: Nearer My Freedom*

*Students do not need to be up to date on lessons or using the Lit/Mythology lessons to complete this assignment.

Practice
⍞ Materials: Practice Voyage 
 
➜ PRACTICE
Practice Voyage #79-83 p.90-94

➜ WRITE
Finish your comparison paragraph and then edit. Look for sentence structure, misplaced modifiers, subject-verb disagreement, wordiness, spelling, weak words, etc. Write a second draft to correct mistakes and make each word pack a punch. Compare it with your first draft. Which is better? Why?

Practice
⍞ Materials: Practice Voyage 
 
➜ PRACTICE
Practice Voyage #84-88 p.95-99

Exam Week

There are no exams for this course.
assignment_teachersNote:
★ Grammar/Comp: Grade 8 — TERM 3 EXAM QUESTIONS
· Grammar Voyage/Practice Voyage: Is the bracketed phrase prepositional, appositive, gerund, participial, or infinitive?
1. [To navigate accurately] is an important skill.
2. Robert Trotman, [the former captain,] was invited on board.
3. The seagull sailed [over the top] of the ship.
4. [Giving good advice] is a captain's prerogative.
5. [Fallen down completely,] the rusty freighter brought back memories.

Answers
1. infinitive
2. appositive
3. prepositional
4. gerund
5. participle

· Grammar Voyage/Practice Voyage: Is the bracketed part a phrase, an independent clause, or a dependent clause?
6. Rochelle brought the maps, and [we brought the compass.]
7. [Sailing away] was an easy thing for Maria.
8. [As the terns flew over the ship,] we watched intently.
Is the sentence declarative, imperative, interrogative, or exclamatory?
9. Which ship has the cargo you told me about?
10. The tugboat almost hit the pier at the corner of the dock!
11. Go to the marina and buy some fresh vegetables and bread.
12. The cold currents flow past the island to the north.

Answers
6. independent clause
7. phrase
8. dependent clause
9. interrogative
10. exclamatory
11. imperative
12. declarative
